participants.reasoning: The extracted participants description matches the official criteria and sample details, with only minor paraphrasing and no substantive contradictions.
participants.extracted: Eligible participants were adult women from the San Francisco Bay Area with body mass index 25–40 who self-identified as stress eaters and wanted help controlling stress-related eating. Of 322 women screened, 53 met eligibility criteria and enrolled, and 47 were randomized (24 to mindfulness intervention, 23 to waitlist control). The sample was 62% White, 15% Hispanic/Latino, 15% Asian/Pacific Islander, and 9% other; mean age was about 41 years, mean BMI about 31 kg/m2, and mean weight about 85 kg. Participants were premenopausal and generally overweight or obese; women with diabetes, major endocrine disorders, pregnancy, recent postpartum or breastfeeding status, current dieting, prior mindfulness/yoga experience, eating disorders, substance addiction, steroid or antipsychotic use, or other conditions affecting weight or abdominal fat were excluded.
participants.official:
Female participants were recruited through media outlets and flyers posted in the San Francisco Bay Area between November 2006 to March 2007.
Inclusion criteria:
Women with a BMI between 25 and 40
Exclusion criteria:
Medical issues such as diabetes or medication use such as hormonal supplements that could affect weight loss, insulin resistance, or abdominal fat. Postmenopausal women, history of a bilateral oophorectomy, total hysterectomy, or polycystic ovary syndrome, active endocrinologic diso

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in content, structure, duration, home practice, and the shared nutrition/exercise session, with only minor wording differences.
interventions.extracted: A preliminary, novel mindfulness-based intervention was developed drawing on components from Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR), Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT), and Mindfulness-Based Eating Awareness Training (MB-EAT). The program consisted of nine 2.5-hour weekly classes plus one 7-hour silent retreat day after class 6. Participants were instructed in body scan, mindful yoga stretches, sitting meditation, loving-kindness meditation, and the 3-minute breathing space, along with guided mindful eating practices focused on awareness of hunger, fullness, taste satisfaction, food cravings, emotional and eating triggers, self-acceptance, and inner wisdom. Supplemental practices included awareness of negative emotions and loving-kindness/forgiveness toward others. Each session included guided practice, discussion, and review of progress and challenges, and participants were encouraged to do up to 30 minutes per day of formal home mindfulness practice 6 days per week and to practice mindful eating before and during meals. The waitlist control group received the mindfulness program after posttreatment assessments; both groups attended a 2-hour nutrition and exercise information session midway through the intervention.
interventions.official:
Intervention(duration 16 weeks, setting not reported):
A preliminary, novel intervention was developed drawing on components from MBSR, MBCT, and MB‐EAT. The intervention programme consisted of nine 2.5‐hour classes and one 7‐hour silent day of guided meditation practice after class 6. Classes were held on a weekly basis on the weekend. Participants were instructed in the body scan, mindful yoga stretches, sitting and loving kindness meditations as taught in MBSR, and the “3 minute breathing spa

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes list many primary, secondary, psychological, behavioral, adherence, and biological measures, whereas the official record only reports follow-up at 16 weeks for anxiety and weight.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure abdominal adiposity as the primary outcome, assessed with dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) using an abdominal region of interest defined from the upper boundary of L2 to the lower edge of L4 as an indirect estimate of visceral fat, measured at baseline and posttreatment. Secondary anthropometric/body composition outcomes included body weight (kg), waist circumference (cm), and trunk-to-leg fat mass ratio from DEXA, measured at baseline and posttreatment. Psychological and behavioral outcomes were also measured at baseline and posttreatment using self-report scales: mindfulness with the Kentucky Inventory of Mindfulness Skills (KIMS; subscales Observing, Describing, Acting with Awareness, Accepting without Judgment; 5-point scale), body responsiveness with the Body Responsiveness Scale (7-point scale), chronic stress with the Wheaton Chronic Stress Inventory (0-4 scale), perceived stress with the Perceived Stress Scale (0-4 scale), trait anxiety with the State-Trait Anxiety Scale, trait form (4-point scale), and eating behavior with the Dutch Eating Behavior Questionnaire (DEBQ; dietary restraint, emotional eating, and external-based eating; 5-point scale). Treatment adherence was measured during the intervention by class attendance, weekly minutes of formal home meditation practice, and number of meals eaten mindfully each week. Biological outcomes were also planned at baseline and posttreatment: salivary cortisol awakening response and cortisol slope from home saliva samples collected on four days at awakening, 30 minutes after awakening, and bedtime; and fasting morning serum cortisol from venous blood samples.
